does: This survey systematically catalogs recent AI safety research specific to large language models and generative AI systems, organizing safety challenges from a technical computer science perspective. It covers the operational mechanisms of LLMs, documents fundamental constraints and performance-safety trade-offs that emerge as these models scale in parameters, and examines distinct safety risks that arise during LLM research, development and deployment stages. The work synthesizes current understanding of harms and risks particular to generative language models, emphasizing that safety challenges differ meaningfully across these contexts and require tailored theoretical frameworks rather than generic AI safety approaches.
connects: This survey builds on decades of AI safety research while focusing specifically on generative language models, extending classical safety concerns like robustness and alignment to the novel capabilities and failure modes of large-scale transformers. It responds to recent empirical findings documenting emergent risks in ChatGPT and similar systems—including issues around factuality, jailbreaking, and misuse—while connecting to fundamental ML research on model scaling laws, training-inference mismatches, and the behavior of very large neural networks. The work synthesizes insights from prompt injection vulnerabilities, constitutional AI approaches, RLHF alignment methods, and studies of LLM biases and toxicity that have proliferated following the public release of GPT-3 and successor models.
enables: This survey establishes a comprehensive taxonomy that can guide future safety research priorities for increasingly capable language models. By articulating distinct safety challenges across the research-development-deployment pipeline, it enables researchers to identify gaps and propose targeted interventions, whether through novel training procedures, interpretability methods, or evaluation frameworks. The work points toward needs for unified theoretical understanding of safety properties at scale, development of better safety benchmarks tailored to generative models, and integration of safety considerations into the core training process. It supports the emerging field of specialized AI safety research for frontier systems and motivates interdisciplinary approaches combining ML engineering, governance, and social science perspectives.
